event: Austronesian seafarers, likely from the Philippines or eastern Indonesia, settle the Palauan archipelago, becoming the islands' first human inhabitants.
Early Palauan communities establish villages and begin developing the matrilineal clan-based social organization that would characterize traditional society.
Palauans construct extensive terraced earthworks and hill-cut sculptures on Babeldaob, reflecting a populous and organized society.
Stone monoliths and village platforms such as those at Badrulchau attest to a flourishing chiefly culture across the islands.
Two powerful rival confederations centered on the chiefly villages of Koror and Melekeok dominate Palauan political life.
Spanish explorer Ruy López de Villalobos sights islands in the western Carolines, marking early European awareness of the region near Palau.
Shipwrecked Palauans who drifted to the Philippines provide Europeans, via the Jesuit Paul Klein, with the first detailed account and map of the islands.
A Spanish expedition led by Francisco Padilla reaches Palau, making documented European contact with the islanders.
The British East India Company vessel Antelope, captained by Henry Wilson, wrecks off Ulong, leading to close relations with the high chief Ibedul of Koror.
Prince Lee Boo, son of the Ibedul, sails to England with Wilson but dies of smallpox in London the following year, becoming a celebrated figure.
Spain asserts sovereignty over Palau as part of the Caroline Islands, a claim upheld by papal arbitration after a dispute with Germany.
Following its defeat in the Spanish-American War, Spain sells Palau and the rest of the Carolines to the German Empire.
German administrators begin phosphate mining on Angaur, transforming the local economy and importing contract labor.
Japan seizes Palau and Germany's other Micronesian colonies at the outbreak of World War I.
The League of Nations grants Japan a mandate over Palau, which Tokyo administers as part of the South Seas Mandate.
Koror becomes the administrative capital of Japan's entire South Pacific Mandate, growing into a developed colonial town.
Japanese settlers come to outnumber native Palauans as Tokyo intensifies economic development and migration to the islands.
American forces capture Peleliu and Angaur in some of the Pacific War's bloodiest fighting, ending Japanese control of Palau.
Palau becomes part of the United States-administered Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands under a United Nations trusteeship.
The Trust Territory administrative headquarters moves to Saipan, while Palau remains one of its districts.
Palauans reject a draft constitution for a unified Micronesian federation, choosing instead to pursue a separate political status.
Palau adopts its own constitution, becoming the Republic of Palau with the world's first nuclear-free constitution, and Haruo Remeliik takes office as president.
President Haruo Remeliik is assassinated, plunging the young republic into political turmoil.
President Lazarus Salii dies of a gunshot wound amid disputes over the Compact of Free Association with the United States.
Kuniwo Nakamura is elected president and the capital is constitutionally slated to move to Babeldaob.
After repeated failed referendums, Palauans approve the Compact of Free Association with the United States by amending the constitution's nuclear provisions.
Palau gains full independence on October 1 as the Compact of Free Association enters into force, and joins the United Nations as its 185th member in December.
The Koror-Babeldaob Bridge collapses, killing two people and cutting off the airport and water supply to the capital.
A new Japanese-funded bridge replaces the collapsed Koror-Babeldaob span, restoring vital links.
The seat of government officially moves from Koror to a new capitol complex at Ngerulmud in Melekeok State on Babeldaob.
Palau agrees to accept Uyghur detainees released from the U.S. detention facility at Guantanamo Bay.
Palau establishes the world's first shark sanctuary, banning commercial shark fishing in its waters.
The Rock Islands Southern Lagoon is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site for its natural and cultural value.
Palau creates one of the world's largest marine protected areas, designating most of its exclusive economic zone as a no-take sanctuary.
Palau introduces the Palau Pledge, requiring visitors to sign an environmental promise stamped in their passports.
Palau bans reef-toxic sunscreens, becoming one of the first nations to do so to protect its coral ecosystems.
Surangel Whipps Jr. is inaugurated as president and champions climate action for low-lying Pacific island states.
Palau hosts the Our Ocean Conference, spotlighting climate change, rising seas, and marine conservation challenges facing the nation.
Palau signs a renewed 20-year Compact of Free Association agreement with the United States, extending economic and security ties.
